content.text:
A simple group is one that cannot be broken down further—it has no 
proper nontrivial normal subgroups. These are the "atoms" of group 
theory, the building blocks from which all finite groups are constructed.

content.title: Simple Group
content.content:
A group $G$ is **simple** if its only normal 
subgroups are $\{e\}$ and $G$ itself.

content.title: Cyclic groups of prime order
content.problem:
If $|G| = p$ (prime), then $G$ is simple. The only subgroups are 
$\{e\}$ and $G$ (by Lagrange), and both are normal.

content.title: $A_5$ is simple
content.problem:
The alternating group $A_5$ is simple. This is proven by showing that 
any normal subgroup containing one $5$-cycle must contain all $5$-cycles, 
and similarly for $3$-cycles. Any normal subgroup containing a non-identity 
element must therefore be all of $A_5$.

content.title: Non-simple groups
content.problem:
- $\Z_6$ is not simple: $\langle 2 \rangle = \{0, 2, 4\}$ is a 
 proper nontrivial normal subgroup.

- $S_4$ is not simple: $A_4$ is a proper normal subgroup.

- $A_4$ is not simple: the Klein four-group $V_4 = \{e, (1\ 2)(3\ 4), 
 (1\ 3)(2\ 4), (1\ 4)(2\ 3)\}$ is normal in $A_4$.

content.text:
One of the greatest achievements of 20th-century mathematics was the 
classification of finite simple groups. The proof spans tens of thousands 
of pages across hundreds of papers.

content.title: Classification of Finite Simple Groups
content.content:
Every finite simple group is isomorphic to one of:

1. A cyclic group of prime order $\Z_p$

2. An alternating group $A_n$ for $n \geq 5$

3. A group of Lie type (families related to matrix groups)

4. One of 26 sporadic groups
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The largest sporadic group is the **Monster group**, with order
\[
 2^{46} \cdot 3^{20} \cdot 5^9 \cdot 7^6 \cdot 11^2 \cdot 13^3 \cdot 17 
 \cdot 19 \cdot 23 \cdot 29 \cdot 31 \cdot 41 \cdot 47 \cdot 59 \cdot 71
\]
approximately $8 \times 10^{53}$.

content.title: Quotient by Normal Subgroup
content.content:
If $N \trianglelefteq G$ with $N \neq \{e\}$ and $N \neq G$, then 
$|G/N| < |G|$ and $|N| < |G|$. Thus $G$ can be "built" from 
smaller groups.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This leads to the concept of a **composition series**: a chain 
$\{e\} = G_0 \trianglelefteq G_1 \trianglelefteq \cdots \trianglelefteq 
G_n = G$ where each quotient $G_{i+1}/G_i$ is simple.

items: Prove that $\Z_p$ is simple for prime $p$.
Show that $S_n$ is not simple for $n \geq 3$.

Prove that if $G$ is simple and abelian, then $G \cong \Z_p$ 
 for some prime $p$.

The group $A_4$ has order $12$. Find a composition series for 
 $A_4$.

Show that the center of a simple group is either trivial or 
 the whole group.

marginNotes.text:
For the word of God is alive and active. 
Sharper than any double-edged sword, it penetrates even to dividing 
soul and spirit.
